beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: The phase dial argument being half the rotation angle is the piece that clicked for me, but I want to push on observability. You said squaring the wavefunction hides the minus sign, so the 720-degree weirdness feels untestable. Convince me otherwise: (1) describe an interference setup where I rotate only one path by 360 degrees and the minus one actually shows up; (2) show me the exact phase factors on the top and bottom components at 180 degrees; (3) explain whether a spin-one particle would need one turn or two.

props.command: The phase dial argument being half the rotation angle is the piece that clicked for me, but I want to push on observability. You said squaring the wavefunction hides the minus sign, so the 720-degree weirdness feels untestable. Convince me otherwise: (1) describe an interference setup where I rotate only one path by 360 degrees and the minus one actually shows up; (2) show me the exact phase factors on the top and bottom components at 180 degrees; (3) explain whether a spin-one particle would need one turn or two.

beats.narration_text: Turn an electron all the way around — 360° — and it comes back as the negative of itself.

card.headline: Why an Electron Needs Two Full Turns to Come Back

beats.narration_text: A classical vector: turn it once around and it's back. Identical. No change. That's what 'rotation' means for anything you can see and touch.
beats.note: Classical arrow rotating 360° and returning to identical state — CRIMSON

beats.narration_text: An electron has spin-½. Turn it 360° and something different happens. The quantum state picks up a minus sign. It's the negative of itself.

card.headline:
How can 360° rotation change anything?
Every direction looks the same after one turn.

beats.narration_text: For a classical object, rotation by θ around an axis multiplies the state by e^{iθ}. One full turn: θ=2π, factor=e^{i·2π}=1. Unchanged. That is the classical story.
beats.note: Unit circle; arrow at angle θ; θ=2π → factor=1 labeled CRIMSON

beats.narration_text: Spin-½ is different. The rotation operator uses θ/2 — half the angle. So one full turn: θ=2π, factor = e^{i·π} = −1. The state comes back as its own negative.
beats.note: Two arrows: classical at θ, spinor at θ/2; at θ=2π classical returns to +1, spinor to −1

card.headline: Rotation uses θ/2 — the spinor double cover

beats.narration_text: At 360°, the sign flag reads '−'. The state is real but phase-shifted. At 720°, θ/2=2π, the factor is e^{i·2π}=+1 — and the state is finally back to itself. Two full rotations required.
beats.note: Sign-flag diagram: classical arrow + spinor arrow tracked from 0° to 720°; sign −1 at 360°, +1 at 720°

beats.narration_text: This is not just math. In 1975, Werner, Colella, Overhauser, and Eagen split a neutron beam, rotated one path by 360° using a magnetic field, then recombined. The interference pattern shifted by exactly π.
beats.note: Interferometer schematic: beam splitter, two paths, magnetic field on one path, recombination, shifted fringe pattern

beats.narration_text: The minus sign is physically real.

card.headline: The minus sign is physically real — measured in 1975

beats.narration_text: The rotated beam had acquired a minus sign. The unrotated beam had not. The interference between them confirmed it. No classical top, no matter how small, does this. Spin is not rotation of an object — it is something new.

props.headline: The rotated beam had acquired a minus sign. The unrotated beam…

beats.note: STILL·ai: neutron interferometer diagram showing split beam, magnetic rotation region, recombination, fringe shift

beats.narration_text: The classical vector and the spinor live in different rotation spaces. The vector's space closes at 360°. The spinor's space closes at 720°. They are different geometric objects — and nature uses both.
beats.note: Side-by-side: vector loop closes at 360° (CRIMSON), spinor loop closes at 720° (TEAL), Möbius-belt analogy

beats.narration_text: They rotated the neutron spin by θ using a magnetic field for a controlled time. At θ=2π, the interference intensity shifted: I ∝ 1 + cos(θ/2). At θ=0: maximum. At θ=2π: minimum. At θ=4π: maximum again. Exactly as the spinor formula predicts.
beats.note: Graph: I vs θ from 0 to 4π; cosine with period 4π (not 2π); data points at measured values; TEAL curve

beats.narration_text: Spin-½ is not a tiny spinning ball. The rotation operator uses half the angle. One full turn flips the sign. Two full turns restore it. The minus sign is real — measured. That is quantum mechanics.

card.headline:
360° flips the sign. 720° restores it.
The minus sign is measured — not imagined.

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated. They rotated the neutron spin by θ using a magnetic field for a controlled time. At θ=2π, the interference intensity shifted: I ∝ 1 + cos(θ/2). At θ=0: maximum. At θ=2π: minimum. At θ=4π: maximum again. Exactly as the spinor formula predicts. Spin-½ is not a tiny spinning ball. The rotation operator uses half the angle. One full turn flips the sign. Two full turns restore it. The minus sign is real — measured. That is quantum mechanics.

artifactLines: They rotated the neutron spin by θ using a magnetic field for a controlled time.
Spin-½ is not a tiny spinning ball.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [the mechanism just described] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
